metadata.summary: DRY patterns for Terraform: locals for derived values, for_each over count, dynamic blocks for repeated nested structures, and Terragrunt for cross-module config deduplication.

content.title: IaC DRY Patterns
content.summary: Four DRY techniques for Terraform that eliminate duplication without sacrificing readability: locals for computed/derived values, for_each (not count) for similar resources, dynamic blocks for repeated nested structures, and Terragrunt for eliminating provider/backend boilerplate across environments.

why.p: Infrastructure duplication in Terraform manifests as copy-pasted resource blocks, hard-coded tags repeated across every resource, and identical backend/provider blocks across every environment directory. Unlike application code, duplicated Terraform drifts silently — environments diverge when one copy is updated but others are not. The four DRY patterns eliminate each class of duplication at the right abstraction level.

list.item: Three or more identical resource blocks differing only in name/tags — use for_each with a map.
Repeated nested blocks (ingress rules, lifecycle rules, tag sets) — use dynamic blocks.
Values derived from input variables repeated in multiple expressions — use locals.
Multiple Terraform environments sharing the same provider/backend config — use Terragrunt.
Refactoring review: scanning for copy-pasted patterns before a module extraction.
Single-resource modules — DRY adds abstraction overhead with no benefit when there's nothing to deduplicate.
for_each when resource identity must be positional — use count only when order/index is semantically meaningful (rare).
Terragrunt when the project has a single environment — the overhead exceeds the benefit below ~3 environments.

section.name: locals-pattern
section.p: Locals compute values from inputs once and reference them everywhere. They eliminate magic strings, repeated expressions, and environment-conditional logic scattered across resource blocks:

rule.statement: ALL naming conventions, common tag maps, CIDR derivations, and environment conditionals MUST be defined in locals. Resource blocks reference locals, never raw variables or inline expressions.
rule.rationale: Centralizing computed values in locals means a naming change requires one edit; scattered inline expressions mean N edits with N opportunities for drift.

example.title: Locals for naming, tags, CIDRs, and environment flags

section.name: for-each-pattern
section.p: for_each over a map is the canonical pattern for creating multiple similar resources. Prefer it over count because map keys are stable — adding or removing entries does not shift the index of unrelated resources:

rule.statement: Use for_each with a typed map when creating 2+ similar resources. NEVER use count for resources where the set membership can change — count-indexed resources shift when items are inserted or removed, forcing unnecessary destroy/recreate cycles.
rule.rationale: for_each assigns identity by map key; Terraform can add, remove, or update individual items without touching siblings. count-based addressing breaks on any insertion before the last element.

example.title: for_each over a map — S3 buckets factory

antipattern.description: Using count for resources where the list can change.
antipattern.reason: count = length(var.items) means items[0] is aws_s3_bucket.main[0]. Removing item 0 makes item 1 become item 0, triggering a destroy/recreate of an unrelated resource.

section.name: dynamic-blocks-pattern
section.p: Dynamic blocks eliminate repeated nested block structures inside resource definitions — ingress rules, lifecycle rules, tag blocks, and routing rules are the canonical targets:

rule.statement: Use dynamic blocks when a resource has N identical nested blocks that differ only in values. Define the block configuration as a list/map variable; the dynamic block iterates over it.
rule.rationale: Hard-coded repeated nested blocks cannot be parameterized without copy-paste; dynamic blocks make the block set a first-class input to the module.

example.title: Dynamic ingress rules in a security group

section.name: terragrunt-dry-pattern
section.p: Terragrunt DRY eliminates provider blocks, backend configurations, and common inputs from every environment directory. A root terragrunt.hcl generates them automatically from path-derived variables:

rule.statement: Use Terragrunt generate blocks to produce provider.tf and backend.tf in every child directory from path-derived locals (environment, region, account_id). NEVER hand-write backend.tf or provider.tf per environment — any difference between environment configs must be explicit in the root terragrunt.hcl, not duplicated in leaf files.
rule.rationale: A 10-environment project has 10 copies of provider.tf and backend.tf if written manually — the first time an org changes its AWS region or state bucket, 9 of 10 will be forgotten.

example.title: Root terragrunt.hcl generating provider and backend
Module-level terragrunt.hcl — minimal, delegates to root

section.name: llm-prompts-dry
section.prompt:
Identify and eliminate duplication in this Terraform configuration:
[PASTE_TF_CODE]
Look for: 1. Repeated resource blocks that could use for_each 2. Repeated nested blocks that could use dynamic 3. Values that should be in locals 4. Patterns that should be extracted to modules 5. Common configurations across environments
Provide refactored code with explanations.

prompt.purpose: Convert similar resources to Factory pattern with for_each

section.prompt:
Convert these similar resources into a factory pattern using for_each:
Current resources: [PASTE_SIMILAR_RESOURCES]
Create a module that: 1. Accepts a map of configuration objects 2. Creates resources dynamically using for_each 3. Handles optional/conditional attributes 4. Outputs a map of created resources

checklist.check: No copy-pasted resource blocks — common patterns extracted to modules
Locals used for all computed values (name_prefix, common_tags, CIDR derivations)
for_each used instead of count for resources where the set can change
Dynamic blocks used for repeated nested structures (ingress rules, lifecycle rules)
Common tags defined in locals and referenced uniformly
Naming conventions use prefix/suffix variables from locals
Terragrunt generate blocks produce backend.tf and provider.tf (if using Terragrunt)
Module versioning in place — consumers pin to version constraints
